summary: McKenzie shares results from a user survey he ran on Bingo Card Creator using Wufoo, revealing demographic data, feature preferences, and complaints. He A/B tested survey incentivization and found that offering free bingo cards more than doubled response rates versus altruistic appeals. The survey confirmed his customer base is mostly female teachers over 40 and revealed that customers value features he considered too obvious to mention. The number one complaint was that the software isn't free, which he uses as a springboard to explain why charging money actually makes the product better for everyone, including non-paying users.
key_points: Incentivized surveys (offering free cards) crushed non-incentivized ones by more than 2X in response rate, without reducing feedback quality
Displaying the survey prominently in the trial version had zero negative impact on sales conversions

Customers overwhelmingly valued features McKenzie considered too obvious to promote — card uniqueness and customizable word lists — suggesting copy should emphasize what users love, not what creators think is novel
The 80/20 rule applied to support: 80% of customers were comfortable with computers, but 80% of support emails came from the remaining 20%
The top complaint — that the software should be free — actually validates his business model, since charging funds the UX optimization that makes the product worth using
money_quotes: Detailed, actionable advice can be yours if you just ask for it.
I have never had a more lopsided A/B test.
If you want responses, give away free stuff.
The fact that Bingo Card Creator is so easy to use is directly related to the fact that I charge money for it.
The sites that I advertise on do not optimize for their user experience because if their website is better than my textual ad, they don't get paid.
Charging also subsidizes the experience of the 97.3% of trial users who don't actually pay me money.
I don't feel any rancor at folks who believe that everything should be free on the Internet. I just will not accommodate your preferences.
key_insight: User surveys are cheap, don't hurt conversions, and reveal both what customers actually value and how to talk about your product in their language — but only if you incentivize participation.
